front: Work: the ideal city has three classes (workers, auxiliaries, guardians) and is ruled by philosopher-kings under a 'noble lie' dividing citizens into metallic castes
work: The Republic and the Ideal City (Kallipolis)

front: Work: ends with the Myth of Er, in which a dead soldier sees the Spindle of Necessity, watches souls choose lives, and awakes on his funeral pyre
work: The Republic and the Ideal City (Kallipolis)

front: Work: the 'metaphysical critique' of poetry uses the example of God creating the Form of furniture, a carpenter creating furniture, and a painter representing a bed
work: The Republic and the Ideal City (Kallipolis)

front: Philosopher: Leontius's simultaneous desire and disgust upon seeing corpses supports this thinker's theory that the soul has three parts
Work: the tripartite soul is illustrated by a charioteer controlling two horses of opposing natures; also discusses transmigration and divine madness
Philosopher: Plotinus's Enneads, compiled by Porphyry, conceive a cosmology of the One, nous, and the Soul in the tradition named for this thinker
Philosopher: Iamblichus advanced theurgy to achieve henosis, responding to Porphyry in On the Egyptian Mysteries, within the tradition named for this thinker
Philosopher: the Theology of Aristotle, actually a paraphrase of Plotinus, transmitted this thinker's ideas to the Islamic world
Philosopher: Marsilio Ficino revived this thinker's tradition at the Florentine Academy, coined the term for this thinker's namesake form of love, and used prisca theologia
Philosopher: Arcesilaus and Carneades turned this thinker's Academy toward skepticism, promoting reliance on the 'probable' (pithanon) in the face of acatalepsia
Concept: the Quine-Putnam indispensability argument holds that we should have ontological commitments to mathematical entities necessary for our best scientific theories
Concept: Benacerraf's thought experiment about Johnny and Ernie being taught conflicting definitions argues against the mind-independent existence of numbers
Work: the Demiurge creates the universe; four elements correspond to geometric bodies; only dialogue known in medieval times through Calcidius's translation
Philosopher: the Tubingen School (Kramer, Gaiser) argues that this thinker's unwritten doctrines, positing the One and the Indefinite Dyad, underlie all his thought
Philosopher: Quine coined the phrase 'Plato's beard' for the problem of nonbeing: 'nonbeing must in some sense be,' which dulls Ockham's razor
Philosopher: Derrida reinvigorated interest in this thinker's use of khora, a type of space or receptacle
Philosopher: defined man as a 'featherless biped,' which Diogenes the Cynic refuted by plucking a chicken and presenting it at the Academy
